monogenic.effect: APOE ε4 allele — significantly increases lifetime risk of late-onset Alzheimer's
monogenic.note: Combine with rs7412 to determine full APOE genotype

monogenic.effect: APOE ε2 allele — protective against Alzheimer's
monogenic.note: Must combine with rs429358 for APOE ε2/ε3/ε4 classification

monogenic.effect: Factor V Leiden — 5-7x increased risk of blood clots (DVT/PE)
Prothrombin G20210A mutation — 3x increased risk of blood clots
HFE C282Y — primary mutation for hereditary hemochromatosis (iron overload)
HFE H63D — modifier mutation; compound heterozygotes (C282Y + H63D) have elevated risk
LRRK2 G2019S — most common known genetic cause of Parkinson's
GBA N370S — 5x increased risk of Parkinson's; also associated with Gaucher disease
HLA-DQ2.5 proxy — present in ~90% of celiac patients; necessary but not sufficient
HLA-DQ8 proxy — second major celiac risk HLA haplotype

breast_cancer.note: Female-applicable. Risk estimates are for female carriers.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Poor metabolizer allele — reduced metabolism of clopidogrel, SSRIs (escitalopram, citalopram), PPIs
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: Clopidogrel may be less effective; consider alternative antiplatelet therapy. SSRI dosing may need adjustment.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Ultra-rapid metabolizer allele — faster metabolism of CYP2C19 substrates
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: Standard SSRI doses may have reduced effect; PPIs may be less effective at standard doses.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Poor metabolizer allele (most common CYP2D6 LOF) — affects TCAs, codeine, tamoxifen, beta-blockers
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: Codeine may be ineffective (no conversion to morphine); TCA dosing may need reduction.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Reduced statin transport into liver — elevated statin blood levels, increased myopathy risk
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: High-dose simvastatin should be avoided; consider rosuvastatin or pravastatin with lower myopathy risk.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Warfarin sensitivity — requires lower warfarin doses to achieve therapeutic INR
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: Standard warfarin doses may cause over-anticoagulation and bleeding risk. Dose reduction typically required.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Intermediate metabolizer allele — reduced metabolism of warfarin, NSAIDs, and phenytoin
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: Warfarin dose requirements are typically 15–20% lower. Monitor INR closely during initiation.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Poor metabolizer allele — severely reduced CYP2C9 activity
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: Major warfarin dose reduction required (up to 80% lower). NSAIDs may accumulate to toxic levels. Use lowest effective doses.

pharmacogenomics.effect: Loss-of-function allele — combined with *2 creates poor metabolizer phenotype
pharmacogenomics.clinical_guidance: If combined with CYP2C19 *2, full poor metabolizer status — clopidogrel therapy likely ineffective.

carrier_status.effect: Most common CF-causing variant (~70% of CF alleles). Carriers are healthy.
Second most common CF-causing variant in Europeans.
Sickle hemoglobin allele. Carriers (HbAS) are generally healthy with slight malaria protection.
Most common hereditary hearing loss variant in Europeans. Carriers have normal hearing.
Common Tay-Sachs variant in Ashkenazi Jewish population. Carriers are healthy.
GBA L444P — causes severe (type 3) Gaucher disease when homozygous. Carriers are healthy.

nutrigenomics.effect_0: Lactase persistence — tolerates dairy well into adulthood
nutrigenomics.effect_1: Partial lactase persistence — may have mild lactose sensitivity
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Lactose intolerant — reduced lactase production; consider limiting dairy or using lactase supplements

nutrigenomics.effect_0: Fast caffeine metabolizer — caffeine clears quickly; lower cardiovascular risk at moderate intake
nutrigenomics.effect_1: Intermediate caffeine metabolizer — moderate clearance rate
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Slow caffeine metabolizer — caffeine lingers longer; elevated heart attack risk at high intake (>4 cups/day)

nutrigenomics.effect_0: Normal vitamin D-binding protein — typical transport capacity
nutrigenomics.effect_1: Moderately reduced vitamin D transport — consider supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day)
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Significantly reduced vitamin D transport — higher deficiency risk; supplementation recommended

nutrigenomics.effect_0: Normal MTHFR C677T status — standard folate metabolism
nutrigenomics.effect_1: ~35% reduced MTHFR activity — increased methylfolate and B12 needs
nutrigenomics.effect_2: ~70% reduced MTHFR activity — elevated homocysteine risk; methylfolate supplementation recommended over folic acid

nutrigenomics.effect_1: Mildly reduced BH4 synthesis — may affect neurotransmitter production
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Reduced BH4 pathway — may affect dopamine/serotonin synthesis; consider methylfolate

nutrigenomics.effect_0: Normal aldehyde metabolism — no genetic alcohol flush
nutrigenomics.effect_1: Reduced ALDH2 activity — alcohol flush reaction likely; elevated acetaldehyde exposure
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Very low ALDH2 activity — strong flush reaction; significantly elevated esophageal cancer risk with regular alcohol use

nutrigenomics.effect_0: High FADS1 activity — efficient conversion of plant-based ALA to EPA/DHA
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Lower ALA-to-EPA/DHA conversion — direct EPA/DHA from fish or algae oil recommended

nutrigenomics.effect_0: Non-taster — insensitive to PROP/PTC bitter compounds; may enjoy bitter foods more
nutrigenomics.effect_2: Super-taster — very sensitive to bitter flavors; may dislike cruciferous vegetables and dark coffee

mental_health.effect_0: Val/Val — higher COMT activity, faster dopamine clearance. Calmer under stress, lower anxiety baseline, but lower peak working memory.
mental_health.effect_1: Val/Met — balanced dopamine metabolism. Moderate stress resilience and cognitive performance.
mental_health.effect_2: Met/Met — lower COMT activity, slower dopamine clearance. Sharper working memory and focus, but stress-sensitive. Higher anxiety tendency under chronic pressure.
mental_health.note: The 'warrior vs worrier' polymorphism. Neither is better — Met shines in calm, Val shines under pressure.

mental_health.effect_0: Val/Val — full BDNF secretion. Optimal neuroplasticity, strong episodic memory, best antidepressant response to exercise.
mental_health.effect_1: Val/Met — reduced activity-dependent BDNF release. Slightly lower episodic memory. Responds well to aerobic exercise for mood.
mental_health.effect_2: Met/Met — significantly reduced BDNF secretion. Lower episodic memory, higher depression risk under stress. Exercise is especially beneficial.
mental_health.note: Met carriers show greater antidepressant benefit from regular aerobic exercise than non-carriers.

mental_health.effect_0: No Taq1A — normal D2 receptor density. Average reward sensitivity and motivation.
mental_health.effect_1: One Taq1A — ~20% fewer D2 receptors. Lower reward sensitivity; may seek more stimulation.
mental_health.effect_2: Two Taq1A — ~30% fewer D2 receptors. Lower baseline reward signal; elevated risk of reward-seeking behavior and reduced motivation without external stimulation.
mental_health.note: Associated with addiction vulnerability, ADHD traits, and compulsive behavior. Also linked to exercise motivation.

mental_health.effect_0: L/L — high serotonin transporter expression. Resilient to stress, lower anxiety, reduced depression risk.
mental_health.effect_1: L/S — intermediate serotonin reuptake. Moderate stress reactivity.
mental_health.effect_2: S/S — lower serotonin transporter expression. Elevated anxiety and depression risk under chronic stress. Short allele carriers are generally more responsive to SSRIs.
mental_health.note: Environment strongly moderates this variant. Adverse childhood experiences amplify the S/S risk significantly.

mental_health.effect_0: High activity — rapid breakdown of serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine. Generally calmer but may respond less to environmental stimuli.
mental_health.effect_2: Low activity — slower neurotransmitter breakdown. More emotionally reactive and empathetic; elevated aggression risk in adverse environments.
mental_health.note: X-linked gene. Males have one copy; the effect is direct and stronger. MAOA-L in adversity context is the most studied gene-environment interaction.

longevity.effect_0: GG — non-longevity genotype. Average lifespan genetics at this locus.
longevity.effect_1: GT — one protective allele. Moderately associated with longer healthy lifespan.
longevity.effect_2: TT — both protective alleles. Consistently enriched in centenarian cohorts across Japan, Germany, Italy, and the US. Associated with reduced oxidative stress and enhanced autophagy.
longevity.note: One of the most replicated longevity variants in human genetics. FOXO3 is a master regulator of cellular stress response.

longevity.effect_0: CC — lower risk variant. Associated with normal telomere dynamics.
longevity.effect_1: CT — intermediate. Modest association with telomere attrition.
longevity.effect_2: TT — associated with shorter average telomere length and modestly elevated cancer risk (breast, ovarian). Cellular aging marker.
longevity.note: Telomere length reflects cumulative oxidative stress and inflammation exposure.

longevity.effect_0: TT — lowest obesity genetic risk. Normal energy regulation and satiety signaling.
longevity.effect_1: AT — ~1.3× increased obesity risk vs TT. Moderate carbohydrate sensitivity. Benefit from portion awareness.
longevity.effect_2: AA — ~1.7× increased obesity risk vs TT. Increased appetite drive, higher BMI tendency, greater carbohydrate sensitivity. Responds strongly to physical activity intervention.
longevity.note: Most common obesity-associated variant worldwide. Physical activity almost fully abolishes the FTO effect.

longevity.effect_0: GG — lower baseline IL-6 production. Anti-inflammatory tendency. Better cardiovascular aging profile.
longevity.effect_2: CC — higher IL-6 production. Elevated CRP and systemic inflammation. Higher cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and all-cause mortality risk.
longevity.note: IL-6 is a master pro-inflammatory cytokine. Chronic elevation accelerates biological aging.

longevity.effect_0: GG — normal TNF-alpha production. No elevated inflammatory cytokine risk.
longevity.effect_1: GA — higher TNF-alpha production. Moderate inflammatory tendency. Elevated autoimmune disease risk.
longevity.effect_2: AA — highest TNF-alpha. Strongly associated with autoimmune conditions, sepsis susceptibility, and inflammatory bowel disease.
longevity.note: TNF-alpha is a master inflammatory cytokine. Anti-TNF biologics (etanercept, adalimumab) specifically target this pathway.

traits.note: Primary genetic determinant; other genes (OCA2, SLC45A2) also contribute.

traits.effect_0: No MC1R R151C — low genetic red hair signal
traits.effect_1: One MC1R R151C allele — increased red/auburn hair possibility; heightened UV sensitivity
traits.effect_2: Two MC1R R151C alleles — strong red hair genetic signal; significantly increased UV sensitivity
traits.note: MC1R variants also increase sensitivity to UV radiation and anesthetic requirements.

interpretations.TT: Wet/sticky earwax — common in European and African populations
interpretations.CC: Dry/flaky earwax — common in East Asian populations; also associated with reduced body odor
R577R — power/sprint athlete profile; alpha-actinin-3 present in fast-twitch fibers
interpretations.TT: R577X — no alpha-actinin-3; endurance athlete profile; better aerobic training adaptation

traits.note: Olympic sprinters rarely carry TT; endurance athletes have elevated TT frequency.

traits.effect_0: Lower androgen receptor activity — reduced genetic hair loss risk from X-linked locus
traits.effect_2: Higher androgen receptor activity — elevated androgenic alopecia risk
traits.note: X-linked; males have one copy, so effect is direct. Multiple autosomal loci also contribute.
